After the connection is established, import the hierarchy into your SAC Planning model, where it can be used for reporting, planning, and hierarchical navigation.

Step 4: Load the Hierarchy into SAP Analytics Cloud

After creating the G/L Account hierarchy and exposing it through the OData service, the next step is to load the data into the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Planning Dimension using Data Management.

The hierarchy is loaded in three separate import queries.

Query 1 – Load G/L Account Master Data

The first query loads the G/L Account master data along with its corresponding descriptions (texts) into the G/L Account dimension.

This creates the base members of the dimension.

Query 2 – Load Hierarchy Nodes

The second query loads the Hierarchy Nodes and their corresponding Parent Nodes.

This step creates the hierarchical parent-child structure within the dimension.


Query 3 – Load Hierarchy Node Values

The final query loads the Hierarchy Node Value together with the Parent Node mapping.

The Hierarchy Node Value represents the leaf member (in this example, the G/L Account), while the Parent Node defines where the member belongs within the hierarchy.

In other words, the Parent Node establishes the hierarchy levels (Level 0, Level 1, Level 2, and so on) by linking each node to its immediate parent.

Once all three Data Management imports have been executed successfully, the complete G/L Account hierarchy is available in the SAC Planning model and can be used for reporting, planning, filtering, and hierarchical navigation.

Note: The hierarchy is built incrementally. The first import creates the master data members, the second import establishes the hierarchy nodes, and the third import links the leaf members to their respective parent nodes, resulting in the complete hierarchy structure.
We took Node type = L into filter.

Why is the hierarchy loaded in three separate Data Management imports?

At first glance, it may seem that the entire hierarchy could be loaded in a single import. However, SAP Analytics Cloud internally treats master data members, hierarchy nodes, and hierarchy relationships as different entities. Therefore, each of them must be loaded separately.

To understand this, consider the following hierarchy:

  

YCOA

│

└── Balance Sheet

    │

    ├── Assets

    │   ├── Fixed Assets

    │   │   ├── 150000 - Building

    │   │   └── 151000 - Machinery

    │   │

    │   └── Current Assets

    │       ├── 100000 - Cash

    │       └── 101000 - Bank

    │

    └── Liabilities

        └── 200000 - Loan

 

This hierarchy actually consists of two different object types.

The first type is the Hierarchy Nodes, which define the structure of the hierarchy.

Balance Sheet

Assets

Fixed Assets

Current Assets

Liabilities

These are organizational nodes. They do not contain transactional data and do not represent actual business objects. Their purpose is only to organize the hierarchy into different levels.

The second type is the Leaf Members.

100000 - Cash

101000 - Bank

150000 - Building

151000 - Machinery

200000 - Loan

These are the actual G/L Account members that store transactional data. Every posting in the fact table ultimately points to one of these leaf members.

Because hierarchy nodes and leaf members serve different purposes, SAP Analytics Cloud stores them separately.

Import 1 – Master Data

The first import creates only the leaf members.

100000 - Cash

101000 - Bank

150000 - Building

At this point, SAC knows these G/L Accounts exist, but it has no information about where they belong in the hierarchy.

Import 2 – Hierarchy Nodes

The second import creates the hierarchy structure.

Balance Sheet

    └── Assets

          ├── Fixed Assets

          └── Current Assets

Here, SAC creates only the hierarchy nodes and their parent-child relationships.

Notice that no G/L Accounts are assigned yet.

Import 3 – Hierarchy Node Values

The final import connects the two worlds.

150000  → Fixed Assets

100000  → Current Assets

200000  → Liabilities

This tells SAC that the leaf member 150000 belongs under the hierarchy node Fixed Assets, while 100000 belongs under Current Assets.

Only after this mapping does the complete hierarchy become available.

YCOA

└── Balance Sheet

    ├── Assets

    │   ├── Fixed Assets

    │   │   └── 150000 - Building

    │   │

    │   └── Current Assets

    │       ├── 100000 - Cash

    │       └── 101000 - Bank

    │

    └── Liabilities

        └── 200000 - Loan

Why doesn't SAC load everything in one import?

The reason is that hierarchy nodes are not dimension members.

A node such as Assets or Balance Sheet is simply a structural element used for navigation and aggregation. It does not receive postings or contain business data.

The leaf members, on the other hand, are actual dimension members (such as G/L Accounts) that receive transactional postings.

By separating these concepts, SAP Analytics Cloud allows the same hierarchy structure to be reused, modified, or extended independently of the underlying master data. It also supports multiple hierarchies over the same set of dimension members, making the model more flexible and easier to maintain.


In this blog, we built a complete G/L Account hierarchy in SAP Datasphere and exposed it for consumption in SAP Analytics Cloud. Although the modeling process is straightforward, understanding the hierarchy structure and selecting the correct fields is essential for successful hierarchy consumption in SAC Planning. In the next blog, we will explore how the same hierarchy can be consumed in a SAP Datasphere Analytical Model, where hierarchy semantics, associations, and consumption patterns differ from the approach used for SAC Seamless Planning.

entry.title: Why Every SAP Analytics Consultant Needs to Learn BDC in 2026 - My Personal Take
entry.content:
Over the past several years, my career has been quietly circled the same problem from different angles. I have configured sales compensation logic across multiple lines of business, implemented SuccessFactors modules, experimented with SAP BTP, written a master's thesis on low-code mobile development, earned an SAC Story Design certification and now spend my weeks living inside SAP Analytics Cloud and Power BI dashboards, with Qlik Sense in the mix too. Different projects, different tools, but really one long conversation between "business needs an answer" and "the data isn't cooperating." 

So when SAP Business Data Cloud started dominating every SAP Community feed and Sapphire keynote this year, I didn't read it as a another product launch to file away. I read it as SAP describing, almost exactly, the job I already do.

SAC Is Becoming Part of BDC And That's Not a Footnote

Here's the part that made me sit up: SAP Analytics Cloud is being folded into Business Data Cloud. SAC keeps its own storage for now, but it's gradually shifting toward BDC's storage layer, particularly Datasphere. That's not a minor backend detail for someone who builds stories and models in SAC but it's a signal that the platform I know is about to sit inside a bigger, more governed architecture and the way I design reports, connect data sources and think about "where does this number actually come from" is going to change with it.

If you're an analytics consultant and your product of choice is SAC, Power BI on top of SAP data or Qlik connected to SAP systems, you're not adjacent to this shift. You're standing right in the middle of it.

The Problem BDC Is Actually Solving Is One I have Lived

Every analytics engagement I have worked on whether it was SuccessFactors reporting, Power BI dashboards for finance or SAC stories has run into some version of the same wall: the data exists but it's scattered across systems, half-governed and everyone has a slightly different version of "the truth." You spend more time reconciling where a number came from than actually analyzing what it means.

BDC's whole premise is that this shouldn't be a per-project problem anymore. Instead of every consultant rebuilding the same data plumbing between SAP systems, Databricks, Snowflake or Big Query, SAP wants that context managed once, centrally, and trusted everywhere. When I first read that framing, it genuinely resonated and it's the exact gap I have spent years working around manually.

Why I'm Not Waiting for "Later" to Learn This

I have written SAP Community blogs before on SAP Build, on connecting BTP trial accounts to Eclipse, and more recently on hands-on impressions of the SAP Analytics Cloud Q1 2026 release. Each time, writing about a tool while I was still learning it forced me to actually understand it, not just use it on autopilot. BDC feels like the next one of those moments, except the stakes are bigger: this isn't a feature update, it's SAP redrawing the map that SAC, Datasphere and BW all sit on.

For consultants, early fluency in a shift like this tends to matter more than fluency in a shift that's already finished. Right now, most people I talk to know BDC exists but haven't gone past the keynote slide. That gap won't last SAP has already flagged that 2026 is the year BDC moves from architecture diagrams into real client pilots. I would rather be the person in the room who's actually poked at it than the person still asking what it is.

What This Means If You're Where I Am

If your background looks anything like mine SAC, Power BI, Qlik, SuccessFactors or general SAP consulting then here's my honest take on where to start:

  • Revisit your SAC projects with a BDC lens: Ask where your data actually lives today and what changes if that storage layer shifts toward Datasphere.
  • Get comfortable with the word "governance" again: BDC is as much a governance story as a technology one and that's traditionally been the less glamorous part of analytics work and which is exactly why it's undervalued and worth learning well.
  • Don't wait for a formal project to start learning: I didn't wait for a client to ask about SAP Build before writing about it. Poke around, read the architecture docs, write down your confusion because that's usually the fastest way through it.

I don't have a finished BDC implementation story to tell you yet but nobody outside a handful of pilot customers really does. What I have is a career built on exactly the problem BDC is trying to solve and enough scar tissue from reconciling data across SAP systems to know this is worth paying attention to early, not after it's already the standard.

If you're an analytics consultant reading this and thinking "I'll get to BDC eventually"  then I'd gently push back. The people who get to it first are the ones who get to shape how their clients adopt it, instead of just reacting to it.

 

I would love to hear from others in the SAP Community , if you've started exploring BDC in your SAC or Datasphere work, what's actually surprised you so far?

Drop it in the comments, I'm collecting real experiences, not just keynote takeaways.😊

entry.title: Certified as an SAP Data Architect? Join the practitioner discussion
entry.content:
Congratulations to everyone who has completed or is working toward the new SAP Data Architect certification. Becoming certified is an important milestone. It shows that you are building the knowledge needed to design scalable, governed, AI-ready data ecosystems and connect architecture concepts to business outcomes. 

But certification is also just the beginning. Once you start applying these concepts in real projects, new questions naturally come up: 

  • How do I translate architecture principles into implementation decisions? 
  • How should I approach governance, metadata, ownership, and data quality? 
  • How do I connect SAP Business Data Cloud with existing SAP and non-SAP landscapes? 
  • How do data architecture choices change when AI, advanced analytics, or data products are involved? 
  • What patterns are other practitioners using in the field? 

That is where the Data Professionals group comes in. The Data and Analytics Learning Group is the right place to learn, prepare, and ask questions about learning content. The Data Professionals group is the next step: a practitioner community for discussing real-world architecture, governance, integration, analytics, AI, and SAP Business Data Cloud scenarios with peers, SAP experts, customers, and partners. 

If you have completed the certification, are preparing for it, or are applying the learning journey in your current role, we invite you to join the practitioner discussion here. Bring your project questions, your lessons learned, your architecture challenges, and your practical experience. 

Why don’t you start with one real-world data architecture question you would like to discuss with other practitioners?

entry.title: Mastering SAP Datasphere Replication Scheduling
entry.content:
Data integration is the heartbeat of any modern data warehouse. In SAP Datasphere, keeping data accurate, timely, and reliable depends largely on how effectively data flows, replication flows, and task chains are automated.

To support a variety of business and technical requirements, SAP Datasphere provides three scheduling approaches that work together:

  1. Frequency-Based
  2. Simple Schedule
  3. Cron Expression

Understanding when to use each method can help organizations simplify operations, improve maintainability, and ensure data is available when the business needs it.

Automated scheduling eliminates manual intervention and ensures data is refreshed consistently. Whether you're loading transactional data every hour, refreshing master data daily, or running complex month-end processes, the right scheduling strategy can:

  • Improve data freshness and availability
  • Reduce operational effort
  • Increase reliability of reporting and analytics
  • Optimize system resource utilization
  • Simplify administration and maintenance

The key is selecting a scheduling approach that matches your business requirements without adding unnecessary complexity.

Frequency-Based Scheduling:

           Frequency-Based Scheduling is the standard method used by most SAP Datasphere users. Instead of creating custom schedules, users simply select predefined execution intervals from the available options.

SAP Datasphere supports:

  • Every 5, 10, 15, 20, or 30 minutes
  • Hourly
  • Daily
  • Weekly
  • Monthly

Benefits

  • Easy to configure No technical expertise is required.

Common Use Cases

  • Hourly Transaction Processing - Load sales orders, inventory transactions, or operational metrics every hour.
  • Daily Master Data Refreshes - Update customer, product, and organizational data during off-peak hours.
  • Weekly Synchronization - Refresh business partner, supplier, or vendor information on a weekly basis.
  • Monthly Data Snapshots - Create period-end financial and operational snapshots for reporting purposes.

Simple Schedule: The Business-Friendly Scheduling Engine

           While Frequency-Based Scheduling defines when jobs run, the Simple Schedule is the SAP Datasphere interface that manages and executes those schedules. Think of it as the user-friendly layer that converts scheduling selections into automated background jobs. Users can configure schedules through a graphical interface without needing to understand technical scheduling syntax.

Key Advantages

  • No Technical Knowledge Required - Business users can create schedules without learning cron expressions or scripting languages.
  • Daylight Saving Time Awareness - Schedules automatically adapt based on the selected regional time zone, helping organizations manage daylight-saving changes.
  • Background Automation - Once configured, jobs execute automatically without ongoing intervention.
  • Easy Maintenance - Schedules are simple to understand and can be easily managed by administrators or business users.

Typical Use Cases

  • Daily persistence jobs at 2:00 AM
  • Weekly replication every Monday and Friday
  • Monthly warehouse refreshes on the first day of the month
  • Standard ETL and ELT workloads
  • For many organizations, Simple Schedule becomes the default scheduling approach because it balances flexibility with ease of use.

Cron Expression Scheduling: Advanced Control and Precision

             Sometimes business requirements go beyond predefined schedules. Perhaps a process must run every 15 minutes during business hours only. Or maybe month-end processing needs to run on the last Sunday of every month. For these advanced scenarios, SAP Datasphere provides Cron Expression Scheduling. Cron expressions allow users to define highly customized execution patterns using a structured scheduling syntax.

Cron scheduling supports:

  • Specific weekdays
  • Multiple executions per day
  • Business-hour-only processing
  • Date range restrictions
  • Month-end scheduling
  • Advanced recurring patterns

Advanced Business Scenarios

  • Business-Hour Processing - Run data loads every 15 minutes during office hours while reducing cloud resource consumption outside business periods.
  • Month-End Refreshes - Execute data warehouse refreshes on the last Sunday of every month.
  • Multiple Daily Executions - Run critical financial updates at: 03:00, 09:00, 15:00
  • This level of flexibility is difficult or impossible to achieve through standard scheduling options alone.

Choosing the Right Scheduling Option :

Business Requirement
Recommended Option
Every few minutes or hoursFrequency-Based Scheduling
Daily, weekly, or monthly loadsSimple Schedule
Business-friendly configurationSimple Schedule
Standard ETLSimple Schedule
Multiple custom run times per dayCron Expression
Complex calendar-based logicCron Expression
Last weekday or last Sunday schedulingCron Expression
Advanced enterprise scheduling needsCron Expression
